data.title: XLF: The Financial Sector At Crossroads
data.description:
American financials have experienced one of the most significant sectoral turnaround stories in recent memory. After living through the scarring effects of the 2008 global financial crisis, years of intense regulatory headwinds, and a period of compressed margins, the US financial sector has re-established itself as a pillar of strength in the broader equity markets, with the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) being the premier way for investors to access the broader US financial system. The US financial sector has managed to navigate the Fed's most aggressive rate-hiking cycle in four decades with stronger balance sheets, record earnings, and, for the first time in years, a regulatory environment that is favorable to the sector. 2026 has, however, provided a reality check for the US financial sector, with XLF substantially underperforming the S&P 500 index so far in the year as macro headwinds and recession risks have impacted the sector.

This article has the purpose of examining catalysts in the near term, monetary policy, deregulation, valuation, and risks. Let's begin.

The Fund

State Street Financial Sel Sec SPDR ETF aims to provide exposure to the broader financial sector of the S&P 500, or, in plain English, the U.S. financial sector. The ETF passively tracks the benchmark, the Financial Select Sector Index. The weighting is market cap based, and around 99% of the holdings are classified as large cap (above $10 billion). The average market cap is $407 billion. The total number of constituents is 76.

Regarding the fund, the expense ratio is extremely low, 8 basis points, and it earns an A+ Seeking Alpha Expense grade. Liquidity is also excellent, with an average daily dollar volume on a 3-month basis around $2.89 billion and an AUM of $48.22 billion, ranking it with an A+ Seeking Alpha Grade. XLF is rated highly in terms of the Dividend Grade also, highlighted by 26 consecutive years of dividend payments, earning it an A- Dividend Grade.

In terms of holdings, almost all the fund is invested in equity, with all of it being, obviously, the Financials industry.

Going deeper, regarding sector allocation within the industry, the majority of the fund is invested in the Financial Services and Banking sector, with around 28% weight each. Capital Markets and Insurance are third and fourth, with Consumer Finance having the residual 4.27% weight.

Regarding ETF Grades in comparison to peer funds, XLF is rated highly and is generally comparable to peer funds. The only category that could be lagging for XLF is Risk, being a notch lower than the group.

The same could be said in relation to comparative price performance on a shorter time scale, while significant differences are present between the fund and other peer funds on a 10-year timeframe.

Deregulation, Conflicting Monetary Policy Effects, and Cautious Credit Conditions

The most pressing catalyst for XLF is the deregulation wave sweeping through U.S. banking regulations. On March 19, the three U.S. banking agencies jointly published two different notices of proposed rulemaking, one to revise the expanded risk-based approach, replacing the previous Basel III Endgame framework, and another to modify the existing standardized approach to cut down on risk weights, remove capital deductions, and make other alterations. This is highly positive for the top stocks in XLF. Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman commented that upcoming proposals would be anticipated to yield a slight reduction in the capital requirements of the biggest banks considering both the Basel III framework and G-SIB surcharge, a sharp contrast from the Basel III framework proposal, which was previously forecasted to have a substantial capital effect.

In the case of the monetary policy environment, the dynamics can be described as having two sides to the story within a short period of time. The Federal Reserve has maintained its current level of federal funds rate at 3.75%, and the continuous rise of headline inflation due to rising energy costs and other implications from the Iran war makes the "higher for longer" approach a probable near-future option, thus presenting an opportunity for banks to earn higher net interest income.

However, this is also accompanied by the increased possibility of economic downturn and loan growth. Banks have enjoyed a high level of profitability with the increased interest rate and hence record net interest income.

Any change in direction by the Fed towards reducing interest rates can lead to reduced net interest income but will, on the other hand, spur loans and capital market activities. This, in my opinion, presents a double-edged sword, with contradictory effects on the bank’s profitability.

In terms of credit, the outlook for the short term is cautious but not dire. The ABA’s Credit Conditions Index held steady at 37.5 for Q1 2026, as it had for five quarters in a row now under the 50 neutral point threshold, indicating that worsening credit conditions were expected for the next six months, although the outlook was stable and showed no worsening.

The Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey conducted by the Federal Reserve in January 2026 reinforces this more nuanced outlook, noting that banks reported tighter lending standards for commercial and industrial loans to firms of all sizes, while also unchanged lending standards and broad weaker demand across all categories of loans. Consumer credit concerns do exist but seem to be kept within reasonable bounds, as total delinquencies increased for Q4 2025, with 4.8% of total debt delinquent, rising slightly for credit card debt, mortgages, and student loans.

Valuation

Going over to the valuation side, the fund looks rather expensive compared to its 10-year P/E average, trading 2 standard deviations above it. The fund is trading at a steep discount relative to the broader market, trading around 14.75x compared to 20.95x.

More importantly, the P/B ratio is 2.16x, not exactly cheap either, at the higher end of historical valuation. The fund is slightly higher traded compared to peers, with IYG trading around 2.09x P/B and FNCL at 2.13x.

From a technical perspective, the fund is trading below its 50- and 200-Day MA, indicating a bearish setup.

Risks

XLF possesses several risks, which should be considered seriously. The main risk is associated with the macroeconomic situation, which may deteriorate the credit quality and hence bank portfolios. Currently, there are upside risks to both unemployment rate and inflation, with stagflation as a risk ahead, which would lead to an increase in problems in already fragile parts of credit markets, i.e., high-yield and leveraged loan corporates and some parts of commercial real estate. In case of a sharp slowing of economic growth, banks would have to deal with even more serious problems than they have now. The risk associated with the concentration of XLF is yet another factor. About 25% of XLF’s portfolio is invested in only two stocks, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and JPMorgan (JPM). This represents significant idiosyncratic risk, and financial sector performance tends to be cyclical in nature, with underperformance during periods of recession risks.

Political and regulatory risk also exists because of the upcoming midterm elections in 2026, when the "affordability crisis" is bound to be a key campaign topic, and if Democrats win control of Congress, there might come an abrupt end to the deregulation period.

Conclusion

XLF presents a benchmark fund to gain exposure to the U.S. financials sector, with above-average Seeking Alpha grades. The current macroeconomic backdrop as well as the regulatory environment could present potential tailwinds for the financial sector in the period ahead, while potential net interest income or loan growth decrease could present challenges ahead. Therefore, the mixed catalysts ahead, paired with the current valuation indicators and the potential risks, lead to my HOLD recommendation.

data.title: IVT: Sunbelt Retail Is Getting Pricey, But Here's My 'Buy Price'
data.description:
InvenTrust Properties (IVT) is my favorite retail real estate investment trust, and I wish I owned a larger position in it.

The REIT is overwhelmingly (97% of the portfolio) concentrated in the Sunbelt region of the United States, and almost 90% of its centers are grocery-anchored. It has modest debt, very few debt maturities over the next few years, high occupancy, affluent surrounding demographics, and best-in-class organic growth rates.

In my last coverage of IVT in October 2025, I rated this Sunbelt retail dynamo as a "strong buy." The stock has performed quite well since then, appreciating about 13% while management recently hiked the dividend by over 5%.

In my view, IVT is extraordinarily well-positioned to maintain its best-in-class fundamental performance in the face of a very favorable environment for high-quality retail real estate.

That said, it appears that even in the best of environments, high-quality retail REITs like IVT still only growth bottom-line cash flow per share by the mid-single-digits. Likewise, the dividend is only likely to grow by mid-single-digits as well -- somewhere in the neighborhood of 4-6% annually.

Back in October, IVT traded at a core FFO multiple (price/core FFO) of 15.2x and an AFFO multiple of about 18.6x. It yielded about 3.4%.

Today, IVT trades at a core FFO multiple of 16.4x and an AFFO multiple of about 20x. It yields 3.15%.

While I love this company and wish I owned a larger position in it, there are good and bad prices to pay for every stock. I would argue that 20x AFFO is too much to pay for a stock growing at a mid-single-digit pace.

Therefore, IVT is a "hold" today.

Update On InvenTrust Properties

Overall, not much has changed with IVT since my October article, so I won't rehash every detail about the company. Instead, I'll try to just touch on some updates.

The biggest update is that IVT sold almost all of its properties in Southern California in order to reinvest into other regions of the Sunbelt, mainly the Southeast.

There may have been a little bit of yield arbitrage between the SoCal portfolio and the properties into which IVT reinvested, but the long-term thesis of this trade is that these other Sunbelt cities will enjoy stronger population and economic growth than the SoCal region in the future, thereby facilitating greater rent growth.

The newly acquired properties mostly look like turnkey investments to me. That is, they look like they are poised to generate robust cash flow for IVT from day one without need for further value-add investment.

If you look up these properties, you will find that they are well-located in generally quite affluent and highly trafficked areas.

This indicates that IVT did not go up the risk curve in order to enjoy some yield arbitrage on the trade. Rather, they sold quality properties in order to reinvest in other quality properties.

With the exception of a few properties in Southern California and Washington DC, the portfolio is now entirely concentrated in what IVT defines as the Sunbelt.

Most of the time, when REITs or other businesses talk about the Sunbelt, they are referring to what we might call the "core Sunbelt" of Arizona, Texas, Florida, Georgia, Tennessee, and the Carolinas. These are the fastest growing, most economically dynamic states within the Sunbelt.

Southern California is debatably part of the Sunbelt, because when people think of the Sunbelt they usually picture the South, and California is its own little world.

Likewise, Virginia is sometimes lumped in with the Sunbelt, giving the region somewhat of a smirk shape, but I personally would not consider it part of the Sunbelt. It's in the Mid-Atlantic region, an area that is culturally and politically distinct from the Sunbelt.

I am starting to sound like a Sunbelt snob (which admittedly I am!), but I am trying to say the quiet part out loud here: When people in the business community talk about investing in the Sunbelt, they don't mean Alabama or Mississippi or New Mexico; they mean the top 15 or so biggest and most dynamic cities in this region.

That's certainly what IVT appears to mean.

I would argue that the Sunbelt snobbery is largely justified, at least in the case of retail real estate.

Let me show you why I think this.

Here's a table showing the same-property net operating income ("SPNOI") growth rates for IVT and some of its retail REIT peers:

2025 SPNOI Growth 2026 SPNOI Growth Guidance (Midpoint)
InvenTrust Properties 5.3% 3.75%
Whitestone REIT (WSR) 4.0% 3.9%
Regency Centers (REG) 5.3% 3.5%
Phillips Edison & Co. (PECO) 3.8% 3.5%
Kite Realty Group (KRG) 2.9% 2.75%
Kimco Realty (KIM) 3.0% 3.0%
Brixmor Property Group (BRX) 4.2% 5.0%

First, note that Brixmor employs a value-add strategy that involves significantly greater capital expenditures than IVT's simpler, cleaner strategy. BRX devoted almost 2x the percentage of NOI to capex from 2021-2024 than IVT did.

That explains BRX's high SPNOI growth.

Unlike REITs like IVT, KIM, and PECO, which spend relatively little on redevelopment, BRX is spending heavily on various capital projects, including redevelopment, in order to push up its SPNOI growth.

Second, note that aside from BRX, the two REITs with highest SPNOI growth in 2025 were both high-quality grocery-anchored ones -- IVT and REG. PECO is also an overwhelmingly grocery-anchored REIT, but its portfolio scores lower in Green Street's TAP (trade area power) rating system, meaning that its properties tend to be middling in quality. That shows up in its slightly lower SPNOI growth rate.

Third, note that, again aside from BRX, the top two REITs for 2026 SPNOI growth guidance -- IVT and WSR -- are both overwhelmingly concentrated in the Sunbelt.

WSR's underlying value was just affirmed when the REIT agreed to be acquired by a real estate fund of Ares Management (ARES) for $19 a share, a more than 12% premium to its previous closing price. That price represents a core FFO multiple of 16.8x and an AFFO multiple somewhere in the neighborhood of 21-22x.

Clearly, ARES sees significant and enduring value in high-quality Sunbelt retail real estate.

The comparison of retail REITs' SPNOI growth rates suggests to me that the confluence of high-quality centers, location in the Sunbelt, and grocery-anchoring is fundamentally the best place to be in this space.

And IVT is right smack dab in the middle of that Venn diagram.

But wait! There's more!

Remember how IVT also boasts one of the strongest and lowest-leveraged balance sheets in the retail REIT sector?

That is still the case.

IVT enjoys the lowest net debt to EBITDA and net leverage ratios in its peer group, and (importantly!) has very little debt maturing over the next ~3 years.

There's a virtuous cycle from maintaining a clean balance sheet. The market tends to reward REITs with lower debt and distant maturities with a higher equity multiple, which lowers the REIT's cost of equity and allows for accretive equity issuance for portfolio expansion. This incrementally increases the REIT's bottom-line growth rate, which reinforces the higher equity multiple, which in turn facilitates more growth, and so on.

That is how IVT can guide for net investment volume (acquisitions minus dispositions) of $300 million this year. Compare that to IVT's total asset value (at cost) of about $3 billion.

Quite simply, the REIT has a cost of capital advantage, and management are using that advantage to grow the portfolio.

Bottom Line

Can you see why I want to own more of IVT?

It is a high-quality, low-risk retail landlord and a reliable compounder of cash flows and dividends.

To demonstrate this, note that, by my simple calculations, 2025's AFFO per share of $1.50 increased 9.5% over 2024's $1.37. That is well north of core FFO per share growth of 5.8% in 2025, because maintenance capex and leasing costs barely budged year-over-year. (Adjusted FFO is basically core FFO minus maintenance capex and leasing costs.)

AFFO per share growth of 9.5% is pretty darn good for a sleepy retail REIT, although I don't expect that level of bottom-line growth to continue going forward.

Instead, I would model AFFO per share growth generally in-line with or slightly above core FFO per share growth. So, if core FFO per share grows at 4-5% per year, I'd expect 5-7% AFFO per share growth.

That is a solid, dependable, recession-resistant growth rate. But that doesn't make it worthy of buying at any price.

My tentative "buy under" price is about $29, which is a 15x core FFO multiple (~18.4x AFFO multiple).

Value investors will scoff that that's still too high a price to pay, but you have to remember that quality comes at a price. You wouldn't expect to buy a Corvette for the same price as a Camry. Neither would you expect to buy the highest quality and fastest growing REIT in its given peer group for the same valuation as the lowest quality and slowest growing names.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Don't Drink The CMS Kool-Aid
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) is a best-of-breed health insurer and pharmacy benefits manager trading at a discount to historical valuations.

However, the insurance segment is under huge pressure from medical cost inflation, which requires them to push up premium rates. Rate increases are, however, curtailed by government funding pressures. This dynamic, which is compounded by a potential deterioration in the health of the insured risk pool, is keeping the medical cost ratio high.

The Q4 2025 results and 2026 guidance announcement coincided with news that the CMS proposal for 2027 Medicare rate increases would be lower than anticipated. The share price dropped up to 20% on earnings day, and continued to drift lower over the 1st quarter, in sympathy with the broader market (SPY).

Then, on April 6th, CMS issued updated 2027 rates for Medicare Advantage, which increased the average rates from the originally proposed 0.09% to 2.48%.

The market liked the news, with an initial share-price gain of over 10%, which dropped back slightly in subsequent trading.

This has prompted a rash of upgrades on Seeking Alpha, with they key theme that the CMS re-rate is a catalyst for both earnings growth and multiple re-rating. The most bullish upside target given was 30-50%.

My thesis is that the impact of the CMS Medicare Advantage re-rating is overstated. The increase lags UNH management's own estimates of medical cost inflation significantly.

Furthermore, as I explain below, the detail of the adjustment clearly shows that this is a one-off for 2027. A major technical re-set is deferred for 2027, but can be expected to apply for 2028.

All in all, the key challenges to the UNH shareholder proposition that are outlined here remain just as problematic for the company as they were prior to this latest news.

I continue to view UNH as the safest bet for investors in managed healthcare, however do not see the CMS re-rating as a cure-all for the company's woes, and as such, suggest that investors take a cautious approach.

The Big Issue For UNH

The Medical Care Cost Ratio

This is the key challenge facing medical insurers, and there are no signs of a solution on the horizon. As shown in this chart from PWC, Medical inflation has been on the rise since 2017, with a brief drop in 2022. PWC estimated for 2026 an average of 8%. The actual level of medical cost inflation recorded by UNH in 2025 was 7.5%, and they guide for 10% in 2026. This is because they have a problem with benefit utilization as well as pure unit cost inflation.

The chart below shows a full cycle of medical cot inflation, in which you can clearly see that medical care costs have significantly exceeded systemic inflation for the last 20 years. The lowest recorded print since 2007 is 5.5%.

This is the key reason that medical insurers faced such a terrible 2025, as cost inflation compressed margins dramatically.

The only solutions for the insurance risk carriers, like UNH to drop the medical cost ratio are to increase premiums, or steer their business away from "frequent flyers" - the people who are sickest and need medical care more often.

This is where the CMS rate reviews come in. Medicare pricing is set by the CMS - Centers For Medicare and Medicaid Services. Rates are set by using actuarial data (which by definition is backward looking) well in advance of the year in question. So the CMS rates announced on April 6th it will apply for the whole of calendar year 2027.

The government knows that it need to keep the system intact, so will authorize enough increases to keep the insurers in the game. However, the political sensitivity of healthcare costs and provision acts to keep a cap on the profitability of managed health insurers.

The Nitty Gritty Of The CMS Update

To understand the impact of the rate increase decision, it's worth reading the CMS announcement which is linked above. The CMS explains in the exhibit below that the actual rate increases applied between the initial proposal and the final number of 2.48% are minimal.

The major adjustment is from "Risk Model Revision and Normalization."

This is explained in the notes appended:

For CY 2027, CMS will continue to use the 2024 MA risk adjustment model which was calibrated with Original Medicare 2018 diagnoses and 2019 expenditures data and fully implemented in CY 2026, in lieu of the updated risk adjustment model calibrated with Original Medicare 2023 diagnoses and 2024 expenditures data that was proposed in the CY 2027 Advance Notice. Continued use of the 2024 MA risk adjustment model will allow the MA market more time to adjust to the recently completed phase-in of the 2024 MA risk adjustment model.

This implies that the adjustment is the result of a short-term deferral of the risk model updates. "More time to adjust" indicates that the update will be made in the next round - effectively nullifying the 2.2% positive impact for future years.

For 2027, the CMS assumption is:

The final policies in the CY 2027 Rate Announcement are projected to result in an increase of 2.48% or over $13 billion in payments to MA plans in CY 2027. When accounting for estimated risk score trend in MA due to factors such as population changes and coding practices, this amounts to a 4.98% increase.

While this is helpful for 2027, UNH management expect to see 10% medical cost inflation in 2026. Even if by 2027 the cost inflation moderates back to say 7.5%, the medical cost ratio will be under huge pressure.

The mechanics of this are that the claims inflation over the 2 years would be 17.5% from the 2025 baseline, while the actual premium increases amount to around half that, after accounting for the modelled benefits of population and coding changes. Bear in mind that these models are run by the CMS - "no guarantee of future performance."

Consider also that the lowest medical cost inflation rate in the last 20 years was 5.5%.

The Medicare Segment

In the UNH disclosures, the Medicare segment is combined with the Retirement business for reporting purposes, accounting for about half of the overall UnitedHealthcare revenues, with $171bn of revenues. The adjustment would add about $4bn of revenues for 2027 (discounting the retirement sector portion, which overstates the number.)

This is great on the surface, but, all things being equal, but would still leave a significant gap to cover the claims costs. Sure, UNH could opt out of coverage for the "worst" counties - but this would reduce the overall premium base. Bearing in mind the CMS rates are actuarially based, and the 2.48% is an average - the bigger rate increases should flow to the 'worst' counties.

And, to top this, the rate increase is, as discussed above, likely to disappear after 2027.

None of this makes a compelling case for dramatic margin expansion and multiple re-rating.

High Hopes for Optum

There has been little news for a while on Optum, the service based part of the business.

The Optum PBM turnaround

Optum Health is undergoing a massive turnaround in the Value Based Care segment. The challenges in profitability stemmed from having grown too aggressively.

UNH is making a significant repositioning here, with a reduction of the partnership footprint to seek restored profitability.

The application of a new risk scoring and payment regulatory mechanism called v28 caused significant margin erosion, and Optum is having to renegotiate partner contracts to mitigate this.

2025 adjusted margins were 4.5%, pulled down by value-based care margins of just 1.7%. Medicare funding cuts are identified as a headwind.

The long-term margin targets for the business are for margins to improve to 6-8%.

Similarly, the fee based Optum business has been underperforming, and significant work is being done here to turn the business around.

There are high hopes for Optum Insights, where AI applications are hoped to generate significant future revenue streams and profitable growth. The company continues to invest here, where the margins of 20% or more look promising.

Finally, Optum Rx, the PBM, has been performing well, with solid margins and growth. There is still no resolution to the DOJ probe into Optum Rx billing practices. This could have the potential to create headwinds for Optum Rx if the findings are negative, or create the need for changes in the operating model.

In fact, the news here is negative. As discussed here, the DOJ probe is expanding into the area of antitrust, which challenges the integrated model between Optum and the insurance business.

So the Optum part of the story faces both operational and regulatory risks.

A Political Football

Healthcare is a massive issue in the current political landscape. President Trump has clearly targeted the health insurance industry, the Obamacare model, and insurer profitability.

Currently, foreign affairs, especially Iran, have taken the limelight. In my opinion, the president will be keen to resolve the Iran conflict well in advance of the upcoming mid-terms. I expect that in the run up to the mid terms his focus will turn to criticism of the previous administration's policies. I have little doubt that the health system, and companies like UNH will return to the limelight. I expect negative press to keep pressure on the share prices in the segment.

Summary & My Strategy

In conclusion, I believe that the positive momentum around the UNH stock price in the wake of the CMS announcement will dissipate.

UNH faces a number of systemic, regulatory and political challenges that are likely to continue to pressure the business results and market sentiment. I expect the share price volatility to continue, and UNH to continue to trade in a range between $275 and $350.

As discussed in previous articles, I believe that this combination makes UNH a better trading stock than a buy and hold. I am currently long UNH, but short $300 September calls. I am also short Jan $250 puts.

A potential trade today would be a buy/write strategy.

Buy 100 shares at $306. Sell 1 March 19th 2027 $350 call at $26. The option premium yields 8.5%. Expected dividends of 2.9% bring the total return to 11.4% assuming the price remains below $350.

  • If shares are called away, the total return would be in excess of 25%.
  • If the share price drops below $300 again, net of options premiums, the cost base would be $280.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Fade The Copper Rally
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) gapped up about 7% on April 8, jumping from yesterday's close of $60.76 to roughly $65 at the open. The catalyst was a copper price rally driven by easing energy costs and renewed optimism around Chinese manufacturing data. Goldman Sachs conveniently initiated coverage with a Buy rating last week. The stock surged. The crowd piled in. And now, if my two decades of gap trading have taught me anything, it's time to fade this move.

The Gap

Let me be precise about what I'm seeing. FCX closed on April 7th at $60.76. On Apr8 it opened near $65, creating a gap of roughly $4. Looking at the candlesticks, we see a 10% difference from Apr7's low to Apr8's high. The bottom of this gap, or the price level where the gap fills, sits at $61.12.

This is an area gap. The telltale signs are all there: it occurred on news that is positive but not structurally transformative. (This is a copper price rally, not a new mine discovery or an acquisition.) The gap was met with heavy volume at the open, suggesting retail enthusiasm rather than institutional accumulation. And the stock is trading near the upper end of its recent range rather than breaking out of a consolidation pattern.

Area gaps fill. My backtests show this consistently across thousands of instances, and commodity stocks like FCX are among the most reliable candidates for gap fills. The reason is straightforward: copper miners are leveraged bets on a commodity that mean-reverts over short time horizons. When copper spikes, FCX overshoots. When the spike fades - and copper spikes always fade - FCX gives back the excess. The gap fills because the exuberance that created it was temporary.

My backtest on FCX up area gaps of 5%+ when near 52-week highs shows a fill rate above 85% within two weeks, with the median fill time around five trading days. The kurtosis here is what makes the trade attractive: the expected gain on the put side (gap fill to $61.12) is roughly $2.50 per share, while the risk of the stock running further from $65 is limited by the fact that FCX has already failed to sustain levels above $68-$69 twice this year. The 52-week high is $69.75, on February 25. There's overhead resistance, and gravity is on our side.

FCX at These Levels Is Overextended

Even setting aside the gap analysis, FCX at $65 is expensive by almost any fundamental measure. Morningstar has a fair value estimate of $33, which means the stock is trading at roughly twice their modeled value, a level they describe as a 66% premium. Now, I don't necessarily agree with a $33 fair value given copper's structural demand story. Yet the magnitude of the premium tells you something about how much optimism is already baked in.

The Grasberg situation adds another layer. The September 2025 mud rush incident halted production at the Block Cave underground mine, and management expects only 85% of district production to be restored by H2 2026. Indonesia is FCX's crown jewel, and the Grasberg minerals district is one of the largest copper and gold deposits on the planet. Yet they are still running below capacity. The stock is priced as if everything is already fixed. It isn't.

On the financial side, FCX earned $0.47 per share last quarter, beating the $0.29 consensus by a wide margin. But that beat was largely a function of higher copper prices ($5.33/lb realized in Q4 versus $4.16 a year earlier), not operational outperformance. Unit net cash costs actually rose 34% year-over-year to $2.22 per pound. When your earnings beat is entirely price-driven and your costs are rising, the sustainability of that beat depends entirely on copper staying elevated. That's a bet, not a certainty, and only experts on copper (of which I'm not) are suited to make such a bet. For me, statistics lead my trades, and the stats say that prices tend to mean-revert.

Why Goldman's Initiation Doesn't Matter Here

Goldman Sachs initiated FCX with a Buy last week, citing higher commodity prices. This is perfectly timed to coincide with the Apr8 gap up setup, which actually is a double gap up (April 1st was the first gap up). Double area gaps are even more reliable fade trades because the second gap tends to be more "dumb money" following hype, with the "smart money" who were already in during the first gap sees an opportunity to make a quick profit by selling at the second gap, and I'm sure it's fueling the enthusiasm.

The dumb money buying this gap are all but destined to get burned, in my opinion. Analyst initiations on commodity stocks near their highs act as lagging indicators, not a leading ones. Goldman is telling you what already happened: copper went up. That's forecasting via rearview mirror - not an actual buy signal.

Now, I'm not making a call on copper's long-term direction here. Copper's structural demand story - electrification, data centers, EVs are real. FCX will probably be a good long-term hold for investors who want commodity exposure. But none of that has anything to do with what happens to the stock over the next five to ten trading days, which is the timeframe for this gap-fill trade.

The Play

Buy the April 2025 $63 puts. These are two and a half weeks out, giving us plenty of time for the gap to fill while minimizing theta decay exposure. At a gap-fill target of $61.12, these puts go roughly $2 in-the-money at expiration, and the intrinsic value movement from $65 to $61.12 nets you the bulk of the profit.

At current premiums, these puts should cost around $2.00-$2.50 per contract. If FCX fills the gap to $62.54 within a week — which my backtests suggest is the median outcome — you're looking at the puts being worth roughly $3.00-$3.50, depending on how quickly it gets there and what happens to IV. That's a 40-50% ROI on a high-probability trade. The max risk is your premium. If copper continues to rip and FCX pushes through $69.75 to new all-time highs, you lose the $2.00-$2.50 per contract. That's a defined risk I'm comfortable with, given the historical fill rates.

I want to be clear: this is a gap trade, not a macro short. If you're bearish copper broadly, there are better ways to express that. This is about exploiting the statistical tendency for area gaps to fill on commodity stocks, which is one of the highest-probability setups in my playbook. The risk/reward favors the bears over this specific timeframe, at this specific price, for the reasons I've outlined above.

Earnings are April 22-23, so the April 25 puts carry some event risk. If you want to avoid that, the April 17 weeklies are an alternative. They come with a tighter timeframe, meaning less room for the trade to develop if the gap takes its time filling. However, they also cost less, so you can buy more (or reduce the capital needed for this trade).

As for me, I'm buying the Apr (monthly) $63 puts.

data.title: CubeSmart: A Historically High Yield, But Still A Hold
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Andoro Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Quick Facts and Why It is Attractive

When I started writing this article, I planned to rate CubeSmart (CUBE)a (cautious) Buy based on price levels and dividend yield. By the time I finished calculating the fair value and evaluating the risks, I ended up with a "Hold" recommendation.

CubeSmart probably needs little introduction to those who follow the self-storage REIT space — it is the third-largest operator behind PSA and EXR, with a tilt towards the Sunbelt market with positive demographic trends.

Let’s remind ourselves of the business fundamentals at a high level. CubeSmart is an established player with 21 years since the IPO. It has an investment-grade BBB rating, and it is internally managed, implying management should be more closely aligned with shareholder interests. Its 15-year dividend growth track record is reassuring: it last cut its dividend in 2008 and 2009, and continued to grow it ever since, even through the COVID pandemic, signaling its resilience. CubeSmart is a US-only business, thus reasonably insulated from global geopolitical shocks (and wars) at an operational level, at least more than most companies that are heavily dependent on global supply chains.

What about its current core metrics that income-focused investors might care about? Debt levels are very manageable: 4.8x Debt to EBITDA with well-staggered debt maturities, and its dividend AFFO payout ratio is 87% — edging high, but in no immediate danger.

Remember, we should use FFO, a standardized metric, and P/FFO to compare peer valuations, but FFO excludes CapEx and thus does not paint the full picture of real cash flow. For the dividend payout ratio, we should use AFFO.

Furthermore, the historical 5-year average P/FFO valuation is ~17x, and the average dividend yield is ~4.6% for the same time period.

Against such a reassuring financial and reputational background, the current ~5.8% dividend yield is at a recent historic maximum, and the valuation of 15x P/FFO is at a historic minimum. Since at least the COVID pandemic, the yield has always gone back down from the 6% mark, mostly due to an increase in the stock price.

It is also worth mentioning that the stock's price has found buyers around the ~$35 level historically.

So this is looking like a great buying opportunity: wait for the price to swing back, and collect a 5.8% yield for 15-20% annualized total returns in the next 1-2 years. This could indeed happen. It seems that Mr. Market is offering investors a generous discount. Sounds too good to be true? Yes, somewhat.

The Catch(es)

No Growth in the Near Term

Mr. Market is generally forward-looking and is sometimes impatient when it sees no near-term growth. The forward P/FFO 14x is pricing in business stagnation. Management guides for a negative 1% same-store NOI growth at the midpoint while expecting a 3.25% to 4.75% same-store expense growth. Income down, expenses up!

This may not come as a surprise for several reasons.

First, CubeSmart's urban/Sunbelt tilt is a double-edged sword — strong long-term demographic tailwinds, but also some of the most competitive supply environments in the country.

Second, management's negative same-store NOI guidance for 2026 isn't arbitrary — it reflects compounding industry headwinds. On the demand side, CubeSmart highlighted that recovery remains gradual, with occupancy still below historical levels and demand normalizing slowly. On the supply side, approximately 19% of CubeSmart's same-store portfolio faces competitive new supply in 2026, down from 24% the prior year, but still a meaningful short-term headwind in key markets. The combination of soft pricing power, supply absorption drag, and sticky cost inflation is what makes the NOI guidance credible.

Don’t Expect Much Dividend Growth Either

Currently, SeekingAlpha shows a 5Y CAGR of 9.46% for dividend growth. This won’t be the case at all in the next 2-3 years.

It is best to assume future growth will be practically flat in the coming years. The company will probably increase by token amounts to keep the growth streak going, but no more. Analyst estimates cap dividend growth at ~3%; anything beyond that pushes the payout ratio into danger territory.

Valuation Isn’t Actually Compelling

The current price of ~$36.5 doesn’t look like a screaming buy when you crunch the numbers. First, let’s go with the P/FFO Reversion model. Given management’s guidance of $2.56 midpoint FFO per share, we get the following target prices in Bear and Base cases:

Bear 13x (Guidance disappoints, valuation contracts further)

Base 16x (valuation goes back up, but no reason to assume historical 17x for 2026)

Bear FFO $2.52

32.76

40.32

Base FFO $2.56

33.28

40.96

The average of these four estimates is $36.83.

At the current price, there is no margin of safety. Given my conservative approach and past experience with guidance falling short, I tend to discount the upper end of projections.

We can cross-check this valuation by estimating fair value using the NAV model. For that, we must look at the latest Financial Supplement:

  • Total portfolio NOI: $771.7 million (annual)

  • Total Debt $3.4 billion

  • Shares: 229 million

  • Implied cap rate ~6.7% (Total NOI $771.7 million ÷ (Market Cap $8.166 billion + Total Debt $3.40 billion)
  • Author's cap rate assumptions: Bear 6.75%, Base 6.5%

Using the formula NAV/Share = (NOI ÷ Cap Rate - Total Debt) ÷ Shares, we arrive at the following fair value estimates:

Cap Rate

NAV/Share

Base 0.065

37.00

Bear 0.0675

35.08

The average of these two estimates is $36.04.

Fair Value Verdict

We have two conservative fair value estimates - $36.83 based on historical P/FFO and guidance, and $36.0 based on NAV.

Given the current price, I do not see compelling value. I agree with Mr. Market’s current valuation. And this is without mentioning further risk factors, some of which lie outside the company’s control.

Geopolitical and Lawsuit Risks

War in the Middle East is pushing the 10Y bond yield higher. This has a direct impact on REIT valuations. Will the war be over soon? It looks like a two-week ceasefire was reached, which gave the stock market a bit of relief. But there are two things to keep in mind. First, a ceasefire isn’t the same as lasting peace, not yet. Second, in complex systems like the global economy, effects take time to show up. The inflationary impact of the past month could still ripple through 2026 in ways that are hard to pin down. We can only hope that it's anything significant, but let’s remember that hope is not a strategy.

Also, lawsuit risk is not captured by the usual metrics, and CubeSmart is now facing one. The New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection filed a lawsuit over predatory practices in the New York market, where CubeSmart has a large presence. When questioned on it on the Q4 2025 call, CEO Christopher Marr offered no specifics:

We're certainly aware of recent announcements, that specific one out of New York. There's been some similar attempts at legislation in other states around not only for storage, but just in general, pricing and transparency. We continue to monitor those and make sure we're in compliance. We are always focused on providing our customers with the optimum experience, and we'll continue to be flexible in terms of focusing in on that and doing that to the best of our ability.

With these additional risks, I don’t think that my fair value estimates are overly pessimistic.

Summary

CubeSmart’s 5.9% yield comes with risks attached that are difficult to ignore. Payout ratio is safe but edging high, no business growth in 2026, bond yield pressures, and a lawsuit to navigate.

This might turn out to be a great tactical trade with 15-20% annualized returns if the price and valuation swing back up to ~$40 based on the P/FFO reversion model, but that hinges on management's promised execution, and the sentiment around the Iran war.

But I cannot in good faith recommend this as a buy for conservative, buy-and-hold investors right now. For those who wish to wait for a better deal, a price of ~$33.5 should present a better entry point, higher yield, and a stronger price support level.

The most risk-averse investors who wish to SWAN (Sleep Well At Night) may want to consider PSA as an alternative self-storage REIT: a balance sheet with an A rating, a very conservative 70% payout ratio, but only a 4.3% yield, and equally very low expected dividend growth in the coming years.

If you are really enticed by the high yield, I believe NNN offers almost the same yield of 5.6%, but with a much safer payout ratio, a lower P/FFO, and some expected growth.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Earnings Offer Catalyst Opportunity For Shares
data.description:
Introduction

One of the world’s largest banks, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), will be one of the first banks to lead off Q1 earnings season next week. The bank’s shares have recently rebounded, mainly due to macroeconomic optimism surrounding a temporary ceasefire in the Iran conflict. Based on the earnings outlook, overall health of the economy, and banking sector, I believe JPMorgan Chase shares are still a buy.

Slight Balance Sheet Growth Expected

Being a large bank, JPMorgan Chase accounts for a significant share of the commercial banking sector’s loans and deposits. Based on previous quarters data, the banks’ changes in both loans and deposits are tightly correlated with how the banking sector is performing. Last Saturday, the Federal Reserve released the last weekly commercial banking report for the first quarter, which gives us some insight into the sector and how the bank may have performed.

In the first quarter, loans in the commercial banking sector grew by just under 1.4%, which is lower than the fourth quarter but well within the range of the last two years. Deposits in the commercial banking sector grew at 1.5% in the first quarter, which was also lower than in the fourth quarter but within the range of the last two years. Based on this, I believe that JPMorgan will show growth of around 2% in lending and around 1% in deposits for the quarter.

Net Interest Income Outlook

While growth in lending and deposits is a good indicator for growth in net interest income, those trends don’t exist in a vacuum. Banks borrow on the short end of the yield curve and lend on the long end of the curve. This is why so many banks had net interest income issues when interest rates rose and caused the yield curve to invert.

For banks, the best yield spread to measure is the 10-year/3-month Treasury spread. While the spread edged higher in January, fell in February, and shot up in March, it averaged 50 basis points for the quarter, and the 45-day moving average was slightly lower. I would consider the overall movement of the Treasury spread to be "Neutral" and combined with the expected growth in loans and deposits, I believe investors can expect higher net interest income for the first quarter.

Analysts Are Also Optimistic

Along with stable interest rate spreads and steady balance sheet growth, analysts have been consistently optimistic about JPMorgan’s earnings outlook throughout the first quarter. While the Iran conflict caused some earnings expectations to be shaved, analysts still increased their expectations for first-quarter earnings by 27 cents, or approximately 5%, over the last 90 days.

For the full years of 2026 and 2027, optimism continues. 2026 full-year earnings expectations have increased by 77 cents, or just under 4%, in 90 days, while 2027 earnings are up 70 cents, or a little over 3%. The push-up of earnings expectations is coming against the backdrop of a share price decline, which means that shares are trading at a lower premium to forward earnings.

The One Risk/Wildcard In Q1 Earnings

While analysts are becoming more optimistic and increasing their earnings expectations, it also places pressure on the bank to perform, as a miss in expectations could cause a selloff and share price volatility. One area that could cause trouble for JPMorgan Chase and its investors is the investment banking arm of the organization.

JPMorgan Chase’s investment banking segment accounts for a large portion of the bank’s total net revenue. In previous quarters, asset management revenue has been a consistent grower, but investment banking fees and principal transactions have not been. In fact, declines in both of the abovementioned caused total net revenue to decline on a quarterly basis in the fourth quarter.

Conclusion

Despite the possibility of headwinds in investment banking, I believe JPMorgan Chase’s depositor banking business will help lead the company to a first-quarter earnings beat. This accomplishment, combined with a lower valuation due to rising earnings expectations, should bring shares higher. I’m still bullish on JPMorgan Chase.

data.title: The Trade Desk Is Now A Deep Value Stock
data.description:
The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) may serve as a clear warning regarding the stock market’s ability to send stocks to the extreme ends of the spectrum. TTD was once priced as a can’t-lose kind of growth engine but is now priced as a deep value stock. The company has plenty of hurdles ahead of it in the form of competition and potential customer turmoil, but the strong balance sheet, robust GAAP profitability, and low valuation make the appeal undeniable. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

TTD Stock Price

I last covered TTD in January, where I rated the stock a "Strong Buy" due to the transition to a focus on profitability. The stock has been pummeled since.

While there are understandable reasons behind the decline, I expect the low valuation to eventually matter.

TTD Stock Key Metrics

TTD works with companies to help purchase advertisements on their behalf across the entire web. In the past, this was often framed as implying an expansive opportunity that goes beyond the typical “walled garden” ecosystems of names like Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL). Nowadays, investors appear concerned that the rise of AI has meant that those walled gardens may end up representing more and more of the entire web.

In the most recent quarter, the company generated 14% YoY revenue growth to $847 million, narrowly exceeding guidance of $840 million. The size of that beat, as well as the growth rate relative to the much larger META, arguably left a lot to be desired. The company did generate $400 million in adjusted EBITDA, beating guidance of $375 million and representing a solid 47% margin.

TTD ended the quarter with $1.3 billion of cash versus no debt, representing a strong, bulletproof balance sheet. The company spent $423 million of cash to repurchase stock in the quarter.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see around $678 million of revenue and $195 million of adjusted EBITDA in the first quarter. This represents just 10% YoY revenue growth.

On the conference call, management cited “sustained weakness among some large consumer packaged goods companies” based on “tariff uncertainty and uneven volumes.” Even the bullish investor in me found this explanation puzzling, given that META and GOOGL would, in theory, be facing the same set of circumstances. Management went as far as to dismiss the potential threats from Amazon (AMZN), saying that Google “was a far better competitor than Amazon is today” because AMZN is mainly focused on "selling their owned and operated inventory." I think that, given the deceleration in the numbers, investors might be questioning the continual dismissal of the competitive threats.

The stock has been very volatile, and the escalating competition from the mega-cap tech titans would have been enough to cause that, but there’s more. The company apparently has held early talks with OpenAI to potentially power their ad platform. CEO Jeff Green made an enormous $148 million insider purchase soon after the earnings release. CEO Green also penned a piece discussing the thoughts behind the purchase, noting his belief that they "have the industry’s most advanced AI and the industry’s richest, most refined, objective, and trusted data platform” and that “objectivity matters even more in an AI world.” For a while that appeared to put a bottom on the stock, but the stock has since struggled due to an audit by their largest clients in Publicis that led it to withdraw its recommendation of the adtech services to its clients. Between the ongoing competitive threats as well as the uncertainty from potentially losing significant business with the largest customers, fear may be peaking.

Is TTD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, TTD was trading at just around 10x this year’s earnings. For comparison, the stock’s 5-year average is around 20x sales. This is quite the fall from grace.

Directionally, the multiple compression makes sense, as TTD is no longer the high-growth darling it once was. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain around low-double-digit growth moving forward, but in my opinion these are now looking rather aggressive in light of the projected deceleration in the first quarter.

The formation of this thesis is now more difficult and different than in the past. Before, one might view TTD as being anchored to long-term secular tailwinds such as connected TV. Perhaps one expected the advertising ecosystem beyond the walled gardens to increase over time. I no longer have much confidence in such views, as AI appears to have given the walled gardens methods to increase their moats further. This means that we must do a complete 180 as we switch from viewing TTD as a growth-at-a-reasonable-price kind of stock to a traditional value stock. The Publicis audit might be viewed as a canary in the coal mine, but the skeptic in me finds it unlikely for a company with a long track record and wide-reaching customers to be taken down by this one setback. Perhaps between customer volatility and escalating competitive threats, growth slows down to the low single digits. I would expect TTD to be a typical beneficiary of AI-induced operational efficiencies given the high level of fixed-cost expenses. I expect significant volatility, as I suspect there are still many investors hoping for a return to growth that I am not expecting to take place. I, however, expect the stock to perform strongly as investors warm up to the potential margin expansion and undervaluation of the stock (which the company is taking advantage of through the share repurchase program). I have a price target of $35 per share, which, while being far lower than my prior target of $55 per share, still represents solid upside. $30 per share would represent around a 17x earnings multiple, which I view to be reasonable given the margin expansion potential as well as the strong balance sheet.

TTD Stock Risks

TTD might be disrupted, perhaps by AI, the mega-cap tech titans, or something else. It is admittedly concerning to see the company’s growth rates decelerate so meaningfully even as the far-larger mega-cap tech titans move in the other direction. It is possible that growth rates not only fail to accelerate but instead end up decelerating, at least in the near term. Furthermore, it is possible that multiples compress if management is unable to offset any pressures to the top line with margin expansion.

TTD Stock Conclusion

There are plenty of potential red flags here. TTD has seen its growth rates plummet, which might be concerning when compared to the accelerating growth rates at the larger competitors. The large insider buy is quite significant even if investors might have already forgotten about it due to the Publicis audit. TTD is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: CoreWeave: Time To Load Up
data.description:
Thesis Summary

In my last article, I upgraded CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) stock to a Strong Buy.

My thesis back then was simple. The market was pricing balance sheet risk, justifiably, but I believed demand would ultimately win.

Since then, two things have happened.

Demand has not just held up, it has actually accelerated. And more importantly, capital markets have remained wide open to fund that demand.

CoreWeave has now passed the survival stage and is in the growth stage.

While other data center stocks have rallied, CRWV has been left behind, but now it’s time to catch up.

The opportunity is still not fully priced, and this remains a window to load up.

The Demand Story Just Broke Higher

The biggest update since my last post is, of course, the expanded agreement with Meta Platforms (META).

This is a $21 billion deal, extending through 2032, with special focus on inference workloads.

$21 billion is nothing to scoff at, but the significance of this goes well beyond the headline number.

The deal confirms what management has been saying for months. Demand is not normalizing or slowing down; it is stepping higher.

Inference is becoming continuous, always-on, and increasingly complex and that change shifts the AI story.

In this regard, CoreWeave is no longer just supplying capacity for model training cycles. It is becoming embedded in the ongoing operation of AI systems.

CoreWeave Is De-Risking

The bear case on CoreWeave has always been straightforward.

Too much capex, funded by too much debt at too high interest and therefore too much reliance on capital markets.

But while this is true, I think CRWV has now proven it can get funding.

CoreWeave has just announced plans to raise $3 billion in convertible senior notes.

Yes, they are unsecured, and of course, there is potential dilution. But this misses the bigger point.

The fact that CoreWeave can raise capital at this scale, in this environment, after everything that has happened in AI and broader markets, is a clear signal.

Moreover, CRWV stock has rallied since the news and has really picked up momentum.

A Virtuous Cycle

Demand is now enabling further long-term contracts, as evidenced by the deal with Meta.

The contract supports financing, potentially at better terms. And this financing helps capture more capacity.

This is an aggressive industrial buildout, but this is exactly what’s needed in the age of AI.

AI infrastructure is being constructed at a global scale, and CoreWeave is positioning itself as one of the key enablers of that buildout.

Furthermore, the take-or-pay nature of these contracts also matters here.

This is not speculative capacity being built in the hope that demand arrives. It is capacity being built against committed demand, with visibility that most infrastructure businesses would envy.

Valuation: Still Not Pricing The End Game

Valuing CoreWeave is not an easy thing to do.

This is not a SaaS company with clean margins and predictable scaling.

It is a capital-intensive infrastructure business, which means traditional multiples can actually be misleading.

Let’s crunch some numbers.

The company is already generating over $1B quarterly revenue run-rate, with backlog now pushing well beyond $70B when including recent deals like Meta.

If CoreWeave can execute toward even a fraction of that backlog over the next few years, we are looking at a multi-billion-dollar revenue business with improving margins as utilization rises.

Now compare that to infrastructure peers.

High-quality data center and infrastructure businesses can trade anywhere between 8x to 15x forward sales.

Nebius (NBIS), which I see as a peer of CoreWeave, now trades at 10x fwd EV/Sales.

Yes, there are many reasons why Nebius deserves a premium to CoreWeave, and I've discussed this before.

However, the differential here is becoming too broad.

Applying a conservative 8x multiple on a $15B–$20B forward revenue scenario gets you to a $120B–$160B valuation.

We could easily be there by the end of 2027.

That’s around 3x today’s price.

Technical Analysis

We held the $70 level multiple times throughout the last few months, and now we may finally be getting ready to turn.

CRWV is looking to break above its 200-day EMA, which could unlock significant upside.

After months of consolidation, we have built a solid base, with technicals low enough so that we can push this back to new highs.

Risks

This is still a leveraged story with significant risk

The convertible raise introduces dilution risk, and the unsecured nature of the debt will reinforce the bear case that this could lead to another private credit crisis.

Execution risk also remains high, and scaling operations of this size comes with significant challenges.

Lastly, customer concentration is another factor. The market is cheering the Meta deal now, but it will react very differently if they suddenly pull the plug.

Final Thoughts

Nothing in the CoreWeave story has broken.

If anything, everything has improved.

Demand is stronger, contracts are larger and longer, capital is still available, and the price is right.

This is no longer about whether AI demand exists. That debate is over.

CoreWeave is becoming one of the key infrastructure layers of the AI buildout, and the once most hated AI stock is becoming one of the most important.

This is the time to load up.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: Stuck In The Dead Zone
data.description:
AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) has struggled getting satellites into space, unlike a couple of big satellite competitors, but it just doesn't matter for the stock price. As long as a major satellite provider leads a hot IPO in 2026, AST SpaceMobile could continue rising. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish due to the stock valuation being vastly disconnected from the corporate execution and the realistic opportunity ahead.

Delayed Satellite Launches

Along with the Q4'25 earnings report on March 3, AST SpaceMobile informed the market of the encapsulation of the BlueBird 7 satellite in February with an expected launch during March. Well, the calendar has already flipped to April, and Blue Origin announced the satellite launch no earlier than April 10. The latest data has already shifted the launch to April 16, or a month and a half delay from when AST SpaceMobile placed an official target on the launch date as late February with no clarification made on the earliest date concept.

A few weeks delay in the space world isn't a big deal unless you're an operator struggling to officially launch services. AST SpaceMobile famously promised customers continuous coverage in the US by the end of 2025, yet the company has only launched one satellite since the 5 small test satellites back in late 2024.

AST SpaceMobile is still pushing for a goal of reaching 45 to 60 satellites in orbit by the end of 2026, but the company is far behind. Satellite launches one to two months apart have been promised for a year now, and the BlueBird 7 is nearly 4 months after the launch of BlueBird 6.

The company is quickly moving into mid-year for the next launch. The BlueBird #4 mission doesn't have a scheduled time yet, and this is one of the key launches where AST SpaceMobile will shift to launching up to 8 satellites at a time.

In addition, AST SpaceMobile has struggled with the launching of individual satellites, so the goal of shifting to stackable satellites is a whole different issue. Just last month, the goal was for Launch 3 to occur during April 2026 with 6 to 8 satellites stacked in one launch vehicle.

Limited Services

The company announced year-end 2025 revenues of $70.9 million, but the vast majority of sales were related to product revenues. AST SpaceMobile delivered 15 gateways during the year to drive the $44.4 million in Product revenues.

AST SpaceMobile only produced $26.5 million in Services revenues in all of 2025. The company reported Q4 revenues of $54.3 million and guided to around $150 to $200 million in revenues for 2026.

The stock already had a market cap of $37 billion despite the failure to truly launch a commercial network. AST SpaceMobile still can't offer direct-to-device services, while T-Mobile (TMUS) has now offered a texting and basic data satellite service for 6 months now.

The market is convinced mobile customers around the globe are going to sign up for T-Mobile Satellite-like services with wireless carriers for upwards of $10/month. The reality is that most consumers don't need the service, and Starlink is going to offer a fully competitive service by the time AST SpaceMobile can offer a contiguous service in just the U.S., much less key markets like Japan or Saudi Arabia for the recent stc deal with a $175 million prepayment.

At the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom conference, CEO Srinivasan Gopalan again confirmed the satellite DTC market is about dead zones, not replacing existing cell services:

When you think of direct-to-cell as a category -- when you think of satellite more broadly as a category. I think broadband is substitutional and it's a good product and it's substitutional because the physics and the economics of it work to be substitutional. I think you heard Gwen and Mike speak this week at Mobile World Congress, and they have the same view, which is direct-to-sell is far more complementary than it is substitutional just because the physics and the economics of it worked that way, whether you think of kind of the beam that you have in a satellite, whether you think about the ability to connect indoors, it's a clearly complementary category. And we like the partnership and we like what it -- the value it brings in terms of putting an end to dead zone.

The consensus analyst estimates forecast AST SpaceMobile basically hitting the 2026 guidance midpoint around $175 million with a massive jump to $785 million in 2027, followed by $1.92 billion in 2028. The stock would still trade over 18x sales of the 2028 revenue targets, while investors shouldn't have this type of confidence in a business that can't even fully launch.

The company has an eventual path to these revenue targets, but one has to question a ramp much beyond these levels when wireless carriers only see satellite DTC services as for dead zone coverage. On the Q4'25 earnings call, the CEO confirmed the $1.2 billion in contractual backlog might amount to only $100 million in annual revenues.

AST SpaceMobile ended the year with $2.8 billion in cash on hand and pushed the pro forma amount to $3.9 billion with an additional capital raise to start 2026. The company is essentially funded for continuous coverage beyond key markets, so capital isn't a worry.

The company has 50 leading global mobile network operator partners with 3 billion subscribers. AST SpaceMobile has the potential to figure out a service plan that meets a larger opportunity than dead zones, but our view still has substantial doubt that most wireless subscribers are going to pay $10 a month for what amounts to an emergency service costing $120 a year.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is investors have rolled into AST SpaceMobile, seeing the constant rise in the SpaceX (SPACE) valuation. AST SpaceMobile might rise due to the hot SpaceX IPO heading towards a $2 trillion valuation, but the stock will ultimately trade based on execution and financials. The company doesn't have the execution to support a price anywhere close to $100.

data.title: Intellicheck: Profitability Is Here, But Growth Must Sustain The Multiple
data.description:
Intellicheck, Inc. (IDN) is a tech company providing identity validation and authentication solutions for KYC, fraud protection, and others. Initially focusing on military and government clients in the 1990s, IDN has expanded its client base over time and today primarily serves financial services and retail clients.

Since its IPO in 1999, the share price has been highly volatile and underwhelming for the most part. From an all-time standpoint, IDN is down 90.6%. Currently trading at $7.8 per share, however, the share price seems to have seen some momentum, as it has been up around 18.5% YTD and has tripled on a 1-year basis, somehow reducing some concern regarding its status as a micro-cap stock. It also seems that the YTD performance has been driven by the 64% increase in share price upon the positive Q4 results in mid-March 2026.

I initiate my coverage with a neutral rating. In my opinion, the 1-year price target of $8.4 per share, which is a potential upside of about 8% from today’s level, may not present a decent risk-reward for investors just yet, though the outlook for IDN remains bullish overall.

Financials Review

Fundamentals have improved overall significantly as of Q4 2025, marked by a shift towards FY profitability and record quarterly revenue. In Q4, IDN delivered a revenue of $6.6 million, a 12% YoY increase, while FY revenue was $22.7 million, a 13% increase YoY. Though the management seems to have mentioned that this was due to stronger SaaS growth, the strong performance in its new verticals, like automotive and title insurance, I think also demonstrates IDN’s ability to scale its business efficiently as it aims to diversify its client industry:

Among the targeted verticals in addition to banking are title insurance, automotive, specialty finance, background screening, logistics and digital account security. Driven in part by these new categories and contract renewals at higher rates, our average price per transaction increased 25% in Q4 versus the previous fourth quarter.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Profitability has turned positive, which I think is the key highlight. In FY 2025, GAAP net income was $1.3 million, while at the same time last year, IDN still saw a loss of -$918k. This has helped FY adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) to jump significantly to $2.6 million, almost a 5x increase from last year’s figure.

The strong bottom-line performance has impacted IDN’s liquidity significantly as of FY 2025. At year’s end, IDN has robust liquidity, with $9.7 million in cash, nearly doubling YoY. A lot of it was driven by the strong operating cash flow (OCF) generation for the FY. In FY 2025, IDN delivered $4.5 million of OCF, a massive swing from the -$2.7 million OCF burn seen last year. As I mentioned earlier, the solid fundamentals further indicate that IDN has turned into a self-sustaining business, greatly minimizing some concerns regarding its micro-cap status, in my view.

Catalyst

I believe catalysts into 2026 would be quite strong for IDN. I think, given the positive turning point we have seen in FY 2025 with double-digit revenue growth, positive GAAP profitability, as well as strong OCF generation, the key question would be, can IDN sustain that kind of performance into the new FY?

So far, I believe there are two forces to evaluate to understand if IDN could deliver as expected. The first one is the favorable macro landscape in IDN’s key verticals, financial services and retail, which the management believes could help sustain demand, as per the comments in Q4:

New business pricing continues to hold firm, and we believe the demand environment remains strong, particularly in financial services and retail, where identity fraud continues to escalate. Our recently published North America identity verification threat report based on nearly 100 million verification transactions in 2025, underscores the critical nature of the problem we solve and the scale of our platform.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Based on my experience covering other identity verification or security-related stocks, I do think that the overall macro trends indeed are favorable for IDN, especially with the rise of generative AI (GenAI) technology that could elevate the risk level in identity verification, primarily in financial services.

My opinion is that GenAI’s capability to potentially produce realistic counterfeit documents and identities at scale means that businesses will continue to face potentially higher costs from fraud losses into the future, further justifying an investment into new, upgraded mechanisms or systems to counter these threats. IDN’s recent focus towards new banking and lending verticals, which almost doubled YoY, as per management’s comment, I think speaks volumes about the growth opportunities due to this driver.

As such, in the end, a double-digit revenue target could still be maintained in FY 2026, based on the demand environment. On that note, with IDN also aiming to continue its cost discipline, I also feel that the company is also in a good position to maintain its overall profit margins.

Risk

Risk is moderate overall, in my opinion. One important thing I found is that IDN seems to have a deep revenue concentration on its top ten customers, as per its 10-K:

For the fiscal years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024, our top ten customers accounted for approximately 77% and 71% of our total revenues, respectively. The loss of any one of these significant customers, or a reduction in their spending levels, could have a material adverse effect on our business, financial condition, and results of operations.

Source: 10-K.

Though revenue concentration could be less of a concern during what I assume to be a rising tide moment for IDN due to the solid demand environment given the elevated fraud threat from GenAI, I believe the sector concentration could become a vulnerability if any slowdown takes place, especially in financial services or retail, two key verticals.

Furthermore, while the outlook is largely bullish, there is also an indication that the stock has probably also realized a lot of its upside over the past month. For instance, I would point to the news of the CEO selling some shares just yesterday, which may be a sign of the stock possibly trading near a temporary high upon the 64% jump the past month.

Valuation/Pricing

My 1-year target price for IDN is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios, based on consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions - I project IDN to deliver a revenue at the high end of the consensus estimate, with the demand environment continuing to strengthen and IDN successfully scaling its banking and lending as well as automotive verticals, followed by solid margin expansions from disciplined cost management. This should help drive re-rating to 7.5x P/S, a valuation premium for IDN.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions - I project some slowdown happening in mid-2026 that does affect some of IDN's client base, delaying contract signing, transactions, and potential renewal discussions. In this situation, I would assume the market would price in the revenue concentration risk, meaning that IDN sees a 6x P/S, just slightly lower than its TTM P/S today, indicating a correction.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $8.4 per share, suggesting around 8% potential upside from the current price level. I give the stock a neutral rating.

Conclusion

The overall picture for IDN is largely positive, in my opinion. There is a big possibility for the company to gradually transition from a niche identity microcap player to a larger player if it continues to maintain its solid performance in FY 2026. In FY 2025, it has delivered double-digit growth as well as GAAP profitability with solid OCF generation, the key things that drive demand for investors.

I give the stock a neutral rating today to be prudent, mainly due to the still moderate risk that could cause a potential correction and the indication that the stock has had a good run and is trading near the top due to the highly solid performance in Q4. Nonetheless, I would not discount the opportunity for IDN to see a surprisingly big jump in share performance at any point into 2026, as we have seen of late, if it continues to perform well into the FY. At this point, despite the 8% projected upside as per my price target analysis, I would remain on the sidelines.
